Project 06 — Capstone

Stock Running Out
in South. Piling Up
in West.

A full supply chain analysis for Norvik Distributors — using SQL to diagnose the problem, Python to forecast next quarter's demand, and Power BI to give procurement a dashboard they can act on every morning.

Norvik Distributors (FMCG Wholesale)

1

SQL — Diagnosing the Stock Problem

Supabase (PostgreSQL) · 4 queries · CASE WHEN, JOIN, CTE, GREATEST

Stock Health & Risk Classification

CASE WHEN
What this shows: Every product-region combination classified as STOCKOUT RISK, OVERSTOCK, or HEALTHY using CASE WHEN logic ordered by urgency. Orange Juice 1L in South surfaces immediately at the top — 0.7 weeks of stock left against a reorder point of 612.

Stock Reorder Recommendations

CTE + GREATEST
What this shows: Exact order quantities per product per region — calculated as (2 months target stock − current stock), floored at zero using GREATEST. Orange Juice 1L South needs 1,534 units ordered immediately. This is a procurement decision, not an observation.
2

Python — Forecasting Q1 2025 Demand

Google Colab · pandas · matplotlib · Weighted seasonal demand model

📓

Norvik_Demand_Forecasting.ipynb

Open the live notebook — all code, outputs and charts viewable in Google Colab

↗

Q1 2025 Forecast by Region

Seasonal Model
What this shows: Forecasted demand for Jan, Feb, Mar 2025 per region. Feb dips in every region — Q1 seasonality working correctly. West consistently lower than South, supporting the SQL finding that West is being over-supplied relative to actual demand.

Top 10 Products by Q1 Forecast Demand

60/40 Weighted Avg
What this shows: White Sliced Bread leads at 6,775 units — yet West has 17 weeks of stock. Orange Juice is third at 5,325 units — yet South has 0.7 weeks. The forecast makes the stock imbalance impossible to ignore.
3

Power BI — Procurement Dashboard

DAX calculated column · Conditional formatting · Slicer interactions · Clustered bar chart

Full Dashboard — Stock Status Table + KPI Cards + Gap Chart

Power BI
What this shows: The complete procurement dashboard. Left: traffic light stock status table — red STOCKOUT RISK, orange OVERSTOCK, green HEALTHY. Top centre: KPI cards showing Orange Juice 1L South with 80 units, 0.70 weeks, reorder point 612. Bottom: gap chart showing all four critical products with current stock vs reorder point side by side.

KPI Cards — Most Critical Product

DAX + Slicers
What this shows: Orange Juice 1L, South — 80 units in stock, 0.70 weeks remaining, reorder point 612, status STOCKOUT RISK. Slicers drive all four cards dynamically. Switch to any product-region combination and every number updates instantly.

Stockout Gap Chart — All 4 Critical Products

Clustered Bar
What this shows: Current stock (light blue) vs reorder point (dark blue) for all four STOCKOUT RISK products. The gap is immediately visible without reading a number. Orange Juice and Full Cream Milk have the most severe gaps — order decisions are obvious at a glance.
💡

The Power BI dashboard is not publicly hosted as interactive visuals require a Power BI Pro licence to share externally. If you'd like to see it live, I'm happy to walk you through it — feel free to reach out and we can arrange a screen share.

Key Findings

What the Data Actually Said

Three findings — two urgent, one structural. Each one has a specific recommended action.

0.7 wks
Orange Juice 1L — South: Imminent Stockout
80 units remaining against a reorder point of 612. At current demand of ~807 units/month, South will run out within the week. Order 1,534 units immediately to restore a 2-month buffer. This is the most urgent action in the entire analysis.
17 wks
White Sliced Bread — West: Severe Overstock
2,355 units in West against average monthly demand of only 393. That's 6 months of stock for a perishable product. West sells less than half the volume of other regions — procurement never adjusted. Pause all West bread orders for Q1 2025.
Same SKU
Chicken Breast: Stockout in North, Overstock in West
North has 1.2 weeks of Chicken Breast left. West has 8.4 weeks. This isn't a supply problem — it's a distribution problem. Stock exists in the network, it's just in the wrong region. A transfer order fixes this faster than a supplier order.
💡 My Insights from This Project

What I Took Away from This Analysis

What I Took Away from This Analysis

1

Why three tools? "Each tool had a specific job. SQL was for diagnosis — joining two years of transaction data to current stock levels to find where the problems were. Python was for forecasting — predicting Q1 2025 demand so procurement could plan ahead, not just react. Power BI was for communication — turning the findings into something a warehouse manager can use every morning without touching a spreadsheet."

2

The SQL approach: "I wrote four queries building in complexity — sales trends, stock classification with CASE WHEN, a JOIN to cross-reference transactions with current stock, and a CTE that calculated exact recommended order quantities using GREATEST to floor at zero."

3

The Python forecast: "I used a weighted average model — 2024 data weighted at 60%, 2023 at 40% — combined with monthly seasonality factors. It's not a complex model but it's defensible and explainable, which matters more in a business context than accuracy to three decimal places."

4

The most interesting finding: "Chicken Breast was in stockout in North and overstock in West simultaneously. Same product, same company, opposite problems. That's not a procurement failure — it's a distribution failure. The fix is a transfer order, not a supplier order. That distinction saves time and money."

5

What I'd recommend: "Three actions: order 1,534 units of Orange Juice for South immediately, pause West bread orders for Q1, and transfer Chicken Breast from West to North. The dashboard makes all three decisions visible without anyone needing to run a query."
